g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Management. We will explore how operating systems create, schedule, and manage processes efficiently.
A process is a program in execution - an active entity with its own memory, state, and resources unlike a passive program.
g.text: A process is a program in execution with its own memory space,
program counter, and system resources. Unlike a static program
stored on disk, a process is dynamic and has a lifecycle.

g.data-narration: A program is a passive file on disk while a process is an active instance in memory with unique PID and resources.
A process memory layout includes text (code), data (globals), heap (dynamic), and stack (function calls) sections.
A process can be in one of five states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, or Terminated based on its execution.
The state diagram shows transitions: New to Ready, Ready to Running via dispatch, Running to Ready on preemption.
The Process Control Block is a data structure that stores all information about a process needed by the operating system.
g.text: The PCB is a data structure that contains all the information the OS needs to manage a process.
It stores process state, registers, scheduling info, memory details, and accounting data.

g.data-narration: PCB fields include Process State, PID, Program Counter, CPU Registers, Scheduling Info, Memory Management, and Accounting.
OS maintains three scheduling queues: Job Queue for all processes, Ready Queue for processes waiting for CPU, and Device Queues.
Three scheduler types: Long-term controls multiprogramming, Short-term allocates CPU frequently, and Medium-term handles swapping.
A context switch saves the current process state to PCB and loads the next process state - pure overhead with no useful work.
g.text: When CPU switches from one process to another:
Duration: typically 1-1000 microseconds depending on hardware support

g.data-narration: In UNIX, fork() creates a child process as a copy, and exec() replaces it with a new program. This forms a process tree.
When a child terminates but parent hasn't called wait(), it becomes a zombie. If parent exits first, child becomes an orphan.
g.text: Parent uses wait() to retrieve child's exit status and release PID

g.data-narration: IPC has two models: Shared Memory (fast, needs sync) and Message Passing (slower but simpler to implement).
Key takeaways: Process is a program in execution with PCB. States: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated. Schedulers manage CPU allocation.
